title: 10 most memorable moments living in the van - Request

transcript.text: Hello, here's a request video I got just yesterday in one of my videos.
It's called 10 Most Memorable Moments of Living in the Van.
So I just typed in just the 10 moments of living in the van that just came to my head first.
So I guess that means most memorable because they just came to my head first.
So I lived in the van from like October of 2010 until January of 2012 and then from like and then like June 2012 until the beginning of 2013.
Um, and, um, so, uh, yeah, the first thing, and not all of these are just are like have directly to do with me living in the van, but I happen to be living in the van when it happens.
So, um, the first one is, uh, there's actually a couple of them from, uh, um, uh, Las Vegas.
The first one that came to my mind was I met this really weird dude in a, uh, Starbucks in Las Vegas.
Um, I was laying there, I was, I was sitting there in a, um, actually I met a lot of weird people in Starbucks in Las Vegas, dude.
A lot of people just start talking to you in Starbucks in Las Vegas, especially this one Starbucks on the western side.
But, um, I'm sitting there and this dude is like starts talking to me.
Um, and, uh, I was, I guess, I mean, I was busy doing stuff obviously because I'm always busy, but for some reason I just, I mean, I just started talking to him.
I allowed him to talk to me and it even distracted for me from my work and, um, for like, and, and he, anyway, he was this like weird dude.
He had like scruffy hair, blonde dude, tall blonde guy, young guy.
And he was telling me that he was a trader for the stock market and he worked on his computer at just two hours a day in the morning and he made $10,000 a month.
But when I looked over at his computer, cause he was on the couch next to me, I saw he was just playing like, uh, an eighties game, like Dilbert or something.
And he had a really old computer and that's all he's doing.
And I should have, the, my ears should have pricked up then, but I was, I told him that I lived in my van and that, and that I was working on my timeline.
He's like, Oh man, I'm going to donate to your timeline.
He was all excited, like acting like a salesperson, like really like overly friendly and like kissing my ass and all this stuff.
And I gave him my phone number cause he's like, yeah, I travel around a lot too.
I have an apartment here in Vegas.
I have an apartment in Hawaii and I'm going to, oh, and you're from Boulder.
I'm going to, I was thinking about getting an apartment in Boulder too.
And I travel around a lot.
So give me your number and I'll give you a call wherever you are cause you travel around.
And I was like, all right, give my number.
And then like, uh, he came in the next day and was like, just like, you know, when people, people are like overly friendly to you, but they're annoying.
Like they come up to you and they, he kept mentioning my name, Kyle pounds, Kyle pounds.
Like I was some kind of like superstar or something.
And, and like fist bumping and being like, yeah.
And then fist bumping me again.
And I was like, and, um, and just, just like not, not acting natural.
And then, so then I went and I was in a library.

Oh, and, but then, and he kept talking about his girlfriend who he had said, you know, just going way into detail about like his sexual experiences with a girlfriend, how he had mushrooms the last night before with his girlfriend.
And he was a teacher and all this stuff.
And then I saw him coming out of another Starbucks at another part of town and he comes up to me and just like, it's like bragging about like he sexed with his girlfriend.
But then he told me he was, he also told me he was gay or bisexual.
But, um, then I was in the library and he calls me up and he's like, he's like, Hey, um, I'm doing, um, I'm, I'm, I'm, I'm investing in something.
You want to chip in, um, to help me invest in one of these stock market things?
I'm like, no.
And then he calls me and then he, um, he, uh, emails me again and he's like, Hey, can I, can I borrow 10 bucks from you?
I'll pay you tomorrow.
And, uh, and I, and I don't think I answered him.
And then he emailed me the next day and he's like, Hey, how about that 10 bucks?
And, uh, yeah, it creeped me out.
He didn't come back to that Starbucks though.
Cause I was there for another like week or something and he didn't, he didn't come back there.
But yeah, that was weird.
Dude, I met a lot of weird people in Starbucks, man.
Come in and talk to me.
Um, in fact, maybe I should just mention all of them.
And this is all in Vegas.
I was in a Starbucks in the Eastern part of Vegas.
This black dude was next to me and this other black dude came in and he was passing out like, uh, um, um, like CDs, sample CDs.
Cause he was a rapper from LA and I was like, what's this guy doing passing out CDs and in Vegas on some, on the far Eastern side.
And, and like, and, but then he gets, he passes it to the dude next to me and the dude next to me was all excited.
And they're talking about it.
And he said he was a, he was a, uh, an agent looking for, for rappers.
I was like, what the fuck?
And he was like, yeah, dude, we'll get together.
And I was like, oh, okay.
But, um, but, uh, he, uh, and then he just suddenly turns to me and he goes, Hey, you should go to the, uh, this, this, uh, this coffee shop over on the Western side.
They're open 24 hours a day.
And then like people are always getting high out there and stuff.
And I was like, really?
And anyways, I ended up going there because he told me to.
And I was like, all right, I guess I'll go.
So I went to this coffee shop on the Western side, which is the more fluent side.
And there was this hippie dude out there and he, and he was like, started talking to me.
And then I got high with him and his friend and we're talking about stuff for a while and he wanted to buy, like thinking about buying my bike frame because I had my road bike frame on my roof.
And, um, yeah, it was kind of funny.
And then at that same Starbucks on the Eastern side, this old guy suddenly starts talking to me about who could barely walk.
He could barely walk.
He was walking like this.
And he, uh, he starts talking to me about, um, like he was really sexist.
And he was just talking like a 16 year old would talk about girls, you know, like, and then, and then Eastern and like all the girlfriends he's had in his life.
And then Eastern, uh, like how the, uh, the Asian women always get in wrecks.
So you don't want to marry an Asian woman.
Cause then she'll keep getting in wrecks and she'll drain your wallet from having to fix the car all the time.
And he said he got injured.
He injured his back in Vietnam war when the wind is like Jeep went over a mine and he blew up and he was flown like a hundred feet.
And I guess that's why he couldn't walk.
But he was like 80 or something.
And then this other like person who I didn't even know, this is the other Starbucks on the Western side where I didn't even know somebody.
I mean, what I didn't even, uh, where I met that crazy dude.
But, um, yeah, this, this, this person is sitting next to me and I couldn't, they were in a, in one of these wheelchairs, movable motorized wheelchairs.
And I didn't know if it was a man or a woman.
It's kind of a portly type of person.
And then all of a sudden they start talking to me and they're like, yeah, I want to do the talent show.
Um, but I need to get on the internet to sign up for it.
And then they said that like three times or four times in a row until I realized they wanted me to do it for him.
Um, so I offered and I, and I signed him up for the talent show.
It was like some kind of like American idol type of thing.
And it turned out to be a woman and she invited me to like hang out with her at her house and stuff.
And then at that same Starbucks, this other guy started talking to me and he was some kind of like, he was, he started talking about some conspiracy theory magazine.
He was talking about like how he liked to listen to like, like David Icke and stuff.
And, and then he would like talk on the phone.
He was some kind of like business, um, advisor or something.
And he'd go and talk on the phone and then he'd leave for like an hour and he'd just be gone and his computer would be there.
And, uh, and finally come back.
And I mean, like, and he had like an external hard drive that he hadn't backed up for like 10 years and he lost all of his information, his like business information on it.
And he was trying to figure out how you like find people who could get the information off of his hard drive.
They didn't have backed up.
And, and then this other Israeli dude started, tried to start talking to me and I, and he was, and I told him about my timeline, but he was kind of probing me, but I didn't really want to talk.
And so he didn't really push me, but, but yeah, man, people, people in Las Vegas are like really outgoing, insanely outgoing.
And then this other dude, I was sitting in a library or a community center area.
And this dude was sitting next to me and started talking to me.
And he lived in a big Winnebago out there in the strip for like 15 years.
And he had a daughter who was going in and like hanging out with him.
He was like 13 or something.
Dude, there are some weird people in Las Vegas.
But anyways, um, so that's the first thing.
Sorry, that took a while, but then I actually have another thing in Las Vegas that just popped to mind.
And that was sleep.
That was, which one was that?
That was, um, number eight sleeping in Hughes field in Las Vegas.
Howard Hughes, I guess owns this big field.
Cause I was sleeping in this big field.
It was like this gravelly field in the Western part of Las Vegas.
And, and I parked out there in the middle of it cause I just liked parking in the middle of like where there's nobody around me.
It's just kind of, it's peaceful.
And I was in this Starbucks and I, there's these cops come in and I came up to the cop and I just asked him what he thought about me sleeping in that field.
Like if it was legal.
And he's like, well, it's Howard Hughes field.
And it's, uh, I mean, technically it's private property, but if nobody's bothering you, then go ahead and do it.
So anyways, that, I just remember that cause I like, it was like sleeping in the middle of a city,
But you get this whole field, giant field to yourself.
Um, okay.
So number two, cops putting me in cuffs.
I got, when I first started living in my van, I was, uh, I showed up at this Walmart and it was, it was a Walmart that you can't camp out in.
But, um, I suited up on my bike.
So I was going to ride my bike to the top of Mount Limit.
And it was like afternoon.
I had to get going.
And, and then all of this, like, literally, literally, like I'm all suited up as I'm.
Closing my door to leave.
My door is sliding before I even managed to close the door.
Like cop car comes up to me.
The cops come up to me and they're like, Hey, you, we have like a, um, mentioning of you.
Like somebody says that you stole somebody's wallet or something like that.
And I was like, really?
I don't know.
I'm in a purse or something like that and bought a bunch of shit.
And I was like, do you mind if we search your car?
And I was like, sure.
So they put me in cuffs in their cop car.
And there was this lady, this like Hispanic lady sitting in a car, like a couple car parking lots over.
And she had borrowed a pin from me like 10 minutes beforehand.
And she never gave it back to me, but I forgot to get it back.
So I was just about to leave.
And, and, um, and, uh, and I was like, and they were searching my car for like an hour.
And there were like three cop cars with like five cops or something.
And, you know, they were searching my rocket box.
It was full.
And I, my car was loaded with stuff.
I had tons of shit in there that they had to sift through.

And I was like, is that lady in the car?
Huh?
And they're like, yeah.
And I was like, well, she has my pin.
Can you get my pin back from her?
So they got my pin.
But anyways, at the end, they decided it wasn't me.
And, uh, and they let me go.
And then, and then like three weeks later, I was in a, I was in a Starbucks in, uh, in Tucson.
And she came up to me.
I didn't recognize her, but she came up to me and she's like, oh, I feel so bad.
I'm sorry for calling the cops on you.
It's just that, um, like, uh, I don't know.
I don't, and, and I was like, well, did they find the culprits or whatever?
She's like, I don't know.
They didn't trust me to, uh, to give advice on who I thought it was after that.
So, but the cops told me that they had like, uh, um, reports that it was a bunch of like
A couple of black dudes were, uh, were seen in the area because somebody bought a bunch
Of stuff at that Walmart with her credit card.
Anyways, that was the second thing that I remembered.
The third thing that came to my mind was freezing my ass off showering in the desert.
When I first started living in my van, I was in Moab and it was in October and it was
Kind of getting cold and I was mountain biking.
Um, and, uh, I was like, I saw, I go mountain biking and then I'd be all dirty and, you know,
I was sleeping in the middle of the desert.
So I had to, I had to have a shower before I sleep and it'd be nighttime and I'd be having
One of those, you know, those hang solar showers, but it was freezing, man.
And just being there like, okay, be strong.
And yeah, if you're going to be camping in the middle of the desert when it's cold,
You got to have some kind of system together where you can have a warm shower.

Was cool.

Parked in the same place all the time.
